ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for ham sui kok; mapped to generic dumpling food product as closest defensible ancestor class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers filled dumplings broadly; no specific code for glutinous rice fried dumplings. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for ham sui kok or equivalent fried glutinous dumpling.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ham sui kok is one piece weighing approximately 55 g, as commonly served in Singaporean dim sum restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Ham Sui Kok — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ham Sui Kok (F06100017). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this canonical Singapore food composition source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glutinous rice and glutinous rice-based products — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice (GI ~98) and mixed fried dim sum dumplings
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for ham sui kok in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glutinous rice has a published GI of approximately 98 (University of Sydney GI Database). The composite GI of 75 is estimated conservatively for the whole dumpling, accounting for the fat and protein content of the filling (lard, char siew, dried prawn) which moderates the glycaemic response relative to plain glutinous rice. GL per serving calculated as: GI (75) × carbohydrate per serving (35.5 g/100g × 55 g serving = 19.5 g) / 100 = 14.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 223 mg/100g with minimal potassium offset and saturated fat contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit ham sui kok to one piece per meal occasion, as the 223 mg sodium per 100 g combined with low potassium content and approximately 6.5 g saturated fat per 100 g can cumulatively elevate blood pressure; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables and avoiding additional soy-based dipping sauces is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI glutinous rice flour base (estimated GI ~75) with 35.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 2.1 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat ham sui kok as an occasional food only, as the glutinous rice flour shell has a high estimated glycaemic index (~75) and delivers approximately 19.5 g rapidly digestible carbohydrate per piece, causing significant post-prandial glucose elevation; if consumed, limit to one piece and pair with non-starchy vegetables and a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (15.6 g/100g) with approximately 6.5 g saturated fat per 100 g from lard and shortening, plus deep-frying generating oxidised lipids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of ham sui kok, as the deep-fried lard-enriched dough contributes approximately 6.5 g saturated fat per 100 g and the frying process generates oxidised lipids and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerosis; a steamed dim sum alternative is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs and the lard-based dough contains pro-inflammatory saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid ham sui kok, as deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation, and the saturated fat from lard and shortening (~6.5 g/100g) further promotes systemic inflammation; steamed dim sum alternatives such as har gao or siu mai are preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ham Sui Kok (咸水角 / 鹹水角, Cantonese: Haam Seui Gok) is a classic Cantonese dim sum item widely served in Singapore and Hong Kong. It is a deep-fried dumpling made from a dough of glutinous rice flour and wheat starch mixed with lard or shortening, giving the shell a distinctive chewy, slightly crispy exterior with a golden-brown, blistered surface. The filling typically contains char siew (Cantonese barbecued pork), dried prawn, diced carrot, and shiitake mushroom, seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar. The combination of the sweet-savoury filling and the chewy fried shell is a hallmark of traditional Cantonese dim sum culture. Nutritionally, ham sui kok is energy-dense at 296 kcal per 100 g, with 15.6 g total fat (approximately 6.5 g saturated), 35.5 g carbohydrate, and only 2.1 g dietary fibre. The glutinous rice flour base has a high estimated glycaemic index (~75), making this a high-GL food unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The sodium content of 223 mg per 100 g is moderate but accumulates quickly when multiple pieces are consumed. Clinically, this food is best treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary component, particularly for those managing blood glucose, blood pressure, or cardiovascular risk.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06100017), which is the primary canonical source for Singapore food composition. The GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for glutinous rice, which is the dominant carbohydrate source; this is documented transparently. Saturated fat is estimated from standard lard/shortening composition for this preparation type and is consistent with the total fat value.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for ham sui kok are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06100017, and no equivalent breakdown is available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific Cantonese dim sum item as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g (6.5 g/100g) is estimated from standard lard and shortening fatty acid composition consistent with the total fat value of 15.6 g/100g; this is not directly reported in the HPB source. 3. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ham sui kok exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. The input record incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' as a dietary tag; this has been corrected to false as the filling contains char siew (pork) and dried prawn. 5. Halal status is false as the filling contains pork (char siew).
